Welcome to the Gary Halbert Letter All-Star Audio series. I'm Kevin Rogers. Joe Sugerman was once called the mail-order maverick by the New York Times. He's a groundbreaker in every sense of the word. His accomplishments in marketing innovation alone give him the right to be called a legend. Sugerman used direct marketing to get the world's first pocket calculator and do as many hands as possible. He followed that success by bringing the cordless phone and the digital watch to the public with his marketing techniques. Sugerman also introduced the concept of using toll-free numbers to take credit card orders over the phone, which was unheard of at the time. Switching gears from electronic products to sunglasses may seem like suicide, but Sugerman was up to the challenge. His direct marketing skills led to the sale of 20 million pairs of blue blocker sunglasses. Sugerman was named direct marketing man of the year in 1979 and received the Maxwell-Sachheim Award in 1991. His books, the AdWheek Copywriting Handbook, and Triggers are considered must reads by anyone in direct response sales. Joe Sugerman, like Gary Halbert, is quick to acknowledge his many failures along the way, but he sums up his career best by saying, the greatest success stories were created by people who recognized a problem and turned it into an opportunity. So appropriately, here's Joe Sugerman reading the Halbert classic Hands-On Experience. Hi, this is Joe Sugerman reading a Gary Halbert letter and it starts out, dear friend and subscriber, every once in a while someone comes to me and says something like this, Gary, I've got to learn how to write copy, I've never done it before and I've got just 30 days to learn how to create a world-class promotion. Can you help me? Can you make me into a world-class copywriter in just 30 days? Can you, huh? Can you, huh, huh? Strangely enough, the answer is yes, well sort of. At least I can give you a qualified yes, an answer to such a question, but actually I may not be able to make someone world-class in just 30 days, but I can almost certainly make some such person better than anyone he or she is likely to be able to hire. Providing, of course, that the person in question has at least a modicum of talent and much more importantly the ability to follow directions and an appetite for very hard work. Here's how I'd do it. If you were my student, the first thing I'd ask you to do is to give yourself a basic education and valid advertising principles. To begin with, I'd want you to read everything listed below, scientific advertising by Claude Hopkins, a Robert Collier, a letter book by Robert Collier, tested advertising methods by John Capels, how to write a good advertisement by Vic Schwab, the Gary Halbert letter, all the back issues by Gary Halbert, of course, the Boron letters by Gary Halbert, the Lazy Man's Way to Riches by Joe Carbo, right through advertising by Eugene M. Schwartz and Seven Steps to Freedom by Ben Suarez. Okay, after you've read all of the above, I would further instruct you to read nothing else and not take notes. You know, getting a good education in any field is tricky and in advertising, it borders impossible. You see, most books written about advertising are not just bad, they are downright dangerous. Many years ago, Claude Hopkins, the greatest admin who ever lived, was asked to critique and offer suggestions on how to improve some college textbooks on advertising. His suggestion? Burn them. Truly, Claude further went on to say that the educators involved had no right to impose such erroneous BS on a group of naive students that it would take years of front-line experience to deprogram the students and free them up from all that garbage. So listen, not only is it important what you learn, it is equally important what you do not learn. So step one is to read only the material I have listed. Now, about this business of not taking notes, don't worry. We're not finished with those books, newsletters, and boron letters after just one reading. Those sir, not by a long shot. Those books and those letters should become your lifetime companions. However, for now, I just want you to just rip right through them and non-stop, okay? Now that you've read all that material, what's next? This, I want you to get a copy of the following ads and direct mail letters. Do you make these mistakes in English? What everybody should know about the Stock and Bond business? The Nancy L. Halbert Herald Green letter. How to burn off body fat hour by hour. At 60 miles an hour, the loudest noise in this Rolls Royce is the ticking of the electric clock. Why men crack? How to collect from social security at any age? The Admiral Bird Transpolar Expedition letter. The Lazy Man's Way to Riches. And in general, anything you can get your hands on that was written by Gary Venega, Dan Rosenthal, Joe E. Kennedy, Pat Gerard, Steve Brown, Drew Kaplan, Claude Hopkins, Joe Carbo, Ben Suarez, Joe Sugarman, Gene Schwartz, and of course, yours truly. Onward. Now that you've obtained copies of these ads and letters, I want you to sit down and copy them word for word in your own handwriting. Next, I want you to create a hand-drawn layout of each ad and direct mail package. Listen, the goal here is to get you to create professional package completely comped up. That is, all ready to go first to a typist and then to a type setter. Now, after you've done all this, I want you to actually take one of these packages to a typist and then to a type setter and have the ad or direct mail package type set. Then proof the ad and after making any necessary corrections, have a V-LOX or a stat made of it. All right, what you have just completed is all the necessary end steps of writing out the final draft of an ad, laying it out and getting a type set had stated and totally and perfectly camera ready so it can be given to a no-brainer publication printer. Do this. Do it. Do it. Don't be simple-minded. Don't come to me and say, okay, Gary, I've got the idea. I know what you're getting at. It really wasn't necessary for me to do all that mechanical stuff as long as I understand what you're driving at. Right, Gary? Sorry, Buck, wait, it doesn't work that way. If you really want to know it, you've really got to do it. There are no shortcuts. You know, I'm sick to death of people who can't be bothered with the little nitty-gritty details of hands-on experience. Now, people who believe that somehow they can know a thing about experiencing it, but listen, it is possible to become conversant with something and really not have any kind of gut understanding of it at all. I'm sorry, but no matter what your mommy or daddy told you, men can never really understand the pain of childbirth. Priests cannot comprehend the joys of sex, and normies can never understand alcoholics. And not one speck of true advertising wisdom has ever been written by a PhD. By the way, did you ever see all those ads by copywriters and DM news and the reporter of direct marketing, the ones where they mention all their awards? No, this. Not one of those that legends mentioned so far in this letter. Care one, iota about awards. No, my friend, if you would ever hear Ben Suarez, Sugarman, or any of the rest of us talking about our achievements, we won't be talking about awards. We'll be talking about numbers. Forgive me, I digress. Let's press on. So far, we've only done the end steps of creating an ad. In actuality, there is a hell of a lot more involved before we ever get to that point. It's time to go back to work. It's time to prepare your toolkit. First, I now want you to go back and reread all those advertising books and back issues of my newsletters, including the bar on letters, and this time, take notes. Write down every good idea, every important insight, and every nugget of wisdom that is contained in all that material. What this means, my friend, is that by the time you are finished, you should have hundreds of notes. Put those notes aside. Next, go back over all the material and write out every headline you find therein. Also, get a bunch of back issues of the National Enquirer and Cosmopolitan Magazine and copy all the headlines you will find that seem to be repeated over and over, especially copy a lot of the cover blurbs from Cosmo. They are superb. Another good source of headlines is 2001 Headlines, which was compiled by J.A. Bram. Let us review. Here's what you should have done so far. One, you should have read all the books and newsletters I have recommended. Two, you should have copied out all the ads and direct mail letters I have listed. Three, you should have at least one of those promotions, copped up and typeset. Four, you should have re-read all the books and newsletters and taken hundreds of notes. Five, you should have read all those headline sources and copied down mucho headlines. Okay, enough review. Next, take all your notes and headlines and put each individual note and each individual headline on a white 3 by 5 index card. And finally, take all those cards and put them in a shoe box and then go take some time off, at least time off from this stuff. Go play golf for a few days and go back to your normal work routine or take a short vacation or whatever. All rested? Guess what? We are now ready after all this prep to begin writing that first ad or direct mail letter. And so let's begin. The first thing I want you to do is read and reread every ad or direct mail package that has already been written about what you are trying to sell. Take notes. Secondly, read and take notes on every ad or direct mail piece you can find that has been written for a competing or similar product or service. Next, carefully examine the product or service and find out everything you can about it. If it's a book, read it. If it's a product, examine it. If it's a service, use it and ask questions. Take notes on all of this. Put those notes on three by five index cards, one note per card and put all those notes in a shoe box. Go do something else for a few days and listen. If you have a good idea during that time, don't verbalize it. Don't write it down. Don't tell anybody and try not to think about it. The idea here is to let everything ferment and boil and bubble up inside you. Back to work. We are now about to write the first draft of our ad. Go isolate yourself in a library or in an office somewhere. Take all your shoe boxes with you. Take out those three by five cards on the product or service you're going to write about and shuffle through those cards. Read them. Say, hmm, every once in a while. Okay. Now, start shuffling through all your other three by five cards. Think about how all those good ideas and insights could apply to your current project. Look at all those hundreds of proven headlines. Think about all those headlines and how they could be modified to work for your current project. Maybe you could change, do you make these mistakes in English? Do you say any of these dumb things every time you call your sock broger? Or maybe Tova Borg-9 swears under oath that a new perfume does not contain an illegal sexual stimulant. That could be transmuted to local jeweler swears under oath that none of those diamonds he sells so cheaply have been stolen. Get the idea. Of course you do. Keep shuffling those cards. Keep reading them. Jot down ideas as they occur to you and actually shuffle the index cards like they were playing cards. Write out a couple of dumb headline ideas. Write out some headlines that make more sense. Write a few that start with how to. Some that start with 17 ways to. And some that begin with an amazing and some that say a little secret that. And so on. Write, write, write, write, write. And guess what? Out of all of this, if you really have done everything I have suggested, exactly as I've instructed, out will pop a central selling idea. So powerful, so fresh, and so compelling that you will know it is exactly right for the ad or direct mail package you are struggling to create. I promise it happens every time. And when it does, write it down. If you are writing a direct mail letter, work that central selling idea into your first sentence. If you are writing any ad, use that CSI in the headline. Now listen up. This is important. What will happen at this point is that your mental floodgates will be wide open. Ideas will come gushing out like water from a broken fire hydrant. Capture those ideas. Forget form, forget grammar. Write, write, write. As fast as you can. Hurry, hurry, hurry. Don't stop for anything. Go, go, go, go. Write, write, write. Page after page. Tell everything. Every detail. Every nuance. Every benefit. Every product feature. Every advantage. Get it all. Get it all. Get it all. Write, write, write, write. Rave, rave, rave. Crow. Describe. Enthus. Give details. Don't worry about getting it perfect. Don't worry about spelling. Don't worry about formulas. Just keep writing. Go fast. Get it all. Write, write, write. And when you are done, set all of this work aside and go do something else for a day or so. Let it cool. And when you are ready to go back to work, I want you to go back to your first draft and now rework it in the following sequence. Number one, say something that gets attention. Number two, tell them why they should be interested. Expand on the CSI. Three, tell them why they should believe what you are saying is true. Four, prove it is true. Five, itemize and describe all the benefits. Six, tell them how to order. Seven, tell them to order now. Okay, now after you have rearranged all your materials so it confirms to the above sequence, you should now check your spelling. Correct your grammar. Edit and in general tighten up your copy. Next, read your copy out loud. When you do this, you will discover all those little stags where your copy isn't smooth, where it doesn't flow well, where the transition from one sentence to another or from one paragraph to another or simply from one thought to another is less than seamless. Now edit again. Make it tight. Use short sentences, short paragraphs, everyday English. Use some one word sentences. Use some one sentence paragraphs. Use subheads that make your copy look interesting and easy to read. You could do more, a lot more. That is not by a long shot all you need to know to write world class copy, but believe it or not, if you just do, I mean actually do it. Everything I've described here, you'll be better than 99% of all those frauds who brag about their golden mailbox and echo awards. And isn't it wonderful how easy it is? Heck, I bet you thought it was going to be work. Sincerely, Gary Halbert, the Raven Maven of marketing. Oh, there's a PS. You want to know what a truly world class, no excuse, no BS copyright or anything like that. You ought to be able to do. It's this. You ought to be able to write an ad that tells you how good he is. Hell, if he can't sell himself, how could you expect him to sell your goods or services? Pray check out the enclosures. Now the enclosures, ad creators collect prizes, ad nauseam, almost ad penitent. Some entries are really bad, including some winners. Judges are hard to find. Joanne Lippmann, staff reporter for the Wall Street Journal. Hollywood has its Oscars, television has its Emmys, Broadway has its Tonys, and advertising has its Clios, and its Andes and Addies and Effies and Obies, and 117 other assorted awards. And those are just the big ones. It seems that the ad business just can't honor itself too much. At scores of black tie gay lads around the country, for instance, sequin and silk await breathlessly white knuckled praying to be tapped for the best beauty ad or the funniest packaged good radio commercial. There are contests for TV ads and radio ads for newspaper, classifies, and billboards. There are so many awards about, oh, about 500 by one count that ad week, a trade publication has started a monthly magazine called Winners just to talk about them. Competitions have multiplied so much that sometimes there aren't enough judges to go around. Never too bad to win. If you want to win awards, there's a show for you. You never too bad to win, says Tom McGelegate, the creative director at Minneapolis based in Fallon, McGelegate, one of the winningest ad agencies. The Press loves awards too, but that's another story. No ad is too small or obscure to be nominated for an award within or without the industry. Consider the Aqua Awards for the best waterbed advertising, the Batchy Awards for the finest state lottery campaign, the Paw Pets are Wonderful awards for non-pet product ads that illustrate responsible pet ownership. In one recent reject, the light bulb commercial in which the light blows out and the women accidentally vacuums up her cat. Yes, fairly, you can read the rest of this article in the 327, 87 edition of the Wall Street Journal. Well, that was a letter written by Gary Halbert. This was, oh, it's got to be in the mid-late 80s, and a lot of things have changed in the first place. And by the way, this is Joe Sugarman. I just finished the ad and saw my name in there a few times. Actually, I've written a book. It's called the Ad Week Copywriting Manual, and it contains everything up to date to the Internet and includes information about the Internet, but it also includes a step-by-step process for writing copy. A lot simpler than Gary's approach. You don't have to read a whole bunch of books. This is the only book you need to read because it actually brings you through the process. It helps you create the sales message. And I've received hundreds and hundreds of emails and Twitter report and Facebook messages that indicated that I have helped many, many people who were struggling with writing copy were able, just from reading that one book, they were able to be successful. Now, in commenting about some of the Gary's philosophies, there are a few in there that are very good. One is you want to read the best direct marketing books, and you want to read them by people who have actually been in the business, not consultants, people who have never really run their own business, but people who have been through it all, people who have been through the business. And another thing you want to do is you want to just keep writing. You just want to, every chance you get, you want to be writing because it's the process of writing, constant writing that build your ability or skill. There's a book by Malcolm Gladwell called Outliers, and he claims in the book that anybody that spends 10,000 hours learning a skill will become a very powerful expert in that field. So writing is very important. He also talks about becoming an expert on a particular subject, on a particular product or service that you're selling, and I can't emphasize that enough. That's one of the first things I do. And very often when you're really going in and delving into a particular product or service and learning everything you can, a big idea pops out. Gary also talks about leaving the work you've just done and go play golf and just do something leisure and forget about it and then come back. That is what I call in my book, The Incubation Process. And believe it or not, your brain works 24-7. I mean, you could be sleeping and your brain is actually working out the details or the things you need to do to your ad to improve it or the big idea that you're trying to come up with. So it's very important that you become an expert and that you incubate, that you let yourself get away from everything and let your brain actually start to work and come up with those brilliant ideas. Gary was a dear friend of mine. I've spoken at a few of his seminars. This letter was written well before my books came out. I know Gary really appreciated those books. I sent him autographed copies. He commented to me a number of times. And I enjoyed appearing at his seminars. He was really a character, a lot of fun to be with. He definitely wasn't boring. And he was really one of the top, one of the country's top copywriters. Since this letter was written, I think the thing that, there are two things that have really changed copywriting. The first one is the computer. Instead of having to sit down and write everything or type everything, of course you've got the computer. It's got spell check. It's even got grammar, way to correct grammar. There are even some programs on how to write copies so that the program itself helps you write. So the computer has really made a big difference and Gary really doesn't refer to that in this particular letter. The second change is the internet. The ability now to test headlines at no cost to test every aspect of an ad color. He talks about paragraph headings to use a lot of paragraph headings and he says that those paragraph headings makes the copy more interesting to read. Actually it makes it less intimidating. It makes it look a lot better. The paragraph headings don't have to say anything that makes sense. I've tested them with paragraph headings that made sense and paragraph headings that had nothing to do with what I was selling. No difference in response. But they are there to break up the ad. There were a lot of very interesting points that Gary brought out. I always read his newsletters. I found them to be so interesting. I subscribed to them initially. Of course then they went on the internet and he developed quite a following. When he passed away it was really, I was really sad about it. I just thought we lost some really great, not just a great copy writer but a great guy and he went way too soon. But anyway, I really appreciate what his sons are doing to help promote Gary's work and to help others. And I'm certainly happy to help both them and to help you. And the best way I can help you is to encourage you to get a copy of my book, which is the AdWee Copywriting Manual, or an older book that I wrote a few years, several years ago called Triggers, which is the psychological triggers you could use to influence and persuade your prospect to buy your product. And then I've written a couple other books, Marketing Secrets of a Male Order Maverick and TV Secrets for Marketing Success. All of these books come from personal hands-on experience where I've actually tested these things. I have failed many times, probably the biggest failure you'll ever hear. But from all this failure and from all the things that I learned, I learned how to perfect my skill. The power of the pen, it's an extremely powerful tool. And if you learn, if you develop the ability to write copy you have within your possession the ability to start businesses, to promote products to the masses, and to help millions of people. I urge you to become an expert not only in the product, but also in the field of copywriting. And I wish all of you the best of success. Thank you very much. This is Joe Sugarman and if you'd like to reach me, you can reach me at BlueBlockerMan. That's B-L-U-B-L-O-C-K-E-R. There's no E in blue. It's BlueBlocker B-L-U-B-L-O-C-K-E-R at AOL.com. And I will look forward to hearing from you if you get a chance. Thank you very much. This is Joe Sugarman saying good evening, or good afternoon, and have a nice day. Bye-bye.
